story:
An invisible line runs down the middle of the backseat, a border Emily and her older brother Mark both respect with the seriousness of diplomats. When Mark accuses her elbow of crossing, Emily insists it’s “on the line,” and they launch into a small-scale negotiation, complete with rules and definitions Mark has picked up from school and Scouts. He declares the line is like a DMZ—neutral territory—while she tests the border with her pink backpack, bulging with “travel essentials”: stuffed animals, a coloring book, yellow plastic binoculars, and a half-eaten pack of Double Stuf Oreos. Their father drives steadily, wedding ring glinting on the wheel; their mother sleeps against the window, glasses sliding down her nose. Without opening her eyes, she murmurs, “Best pie in three counties, then the dog with ears flapping like flags of surrender.” Outside, flat fields unspool into the distance toward Grandma’s house.

The game continues. Mark says Emily’s backpack is her “territory” and therefore her “responsibility.” The border, he claims, is fixed like a constitution; Emily mishears constipation, and their father suppresses a laugh. A pothole jolts the car and Mark’s knee spills over to Emily’s side. He calls it a “temporary violation due to external forces,” drawing a line between accidents and “acts of aggression,” a phrase he defines with the brisk authority of a nine-year-old. Emily tests logic against fairness: if the road can push him over, why can’t her backpack rest where it fits?

Their skirmish quiets as the landscape changes. The fields give way to hills, and the rhythm of the tires threads through the car like a lullaby. When Emily pulls out Mr. Whiskers, her ragged stuffed cat with one button eye loose, Mark softens. Emily says Grandma will fix the eye; Mark reminds her Grandma’s hands shake now. The reminder hangs in the air like a cloud. They pass cows standing motionless in the heat, and a song about leaving and coming home drifts from the radio.

Then, an offer: Mark can fix Mr. Whiskers himself. He learned to sew when he attached a Scout badge, he says; it won’t be perfect. Emily studies him—the freckles, the new glasses, the careful voice—and agrees. Mark crosses the invisible line without comment and takes the stuffed cat gently, turning it in a shaft of sunlight, speaking practically about black thread and a needle he’s sure Grandma can help him find. He returns Mr. Whiskers with care, and Emily lets her elbow spill across the border. This time, he says nothing.

Their mother stirs and settles. Their father reaches over, without taking his eyes from the road, to tuck her hair behind her ear. Emily opens the Oreos quietly and offers Mark the biggest one. They eat as they always have—Mark licking the filling first, Emily saving the middle for last—rituals as old as the road trips themselves. Finally, she saves the last cookie and sets it directly on the line: neutral territory. 

A billboard promises the best pie in three counties. Emily looks at her mother. Out the window, a dog passes with ears flapping like flags of surrender. Emily laughs; Mark taps his foot to the radio. The line remains, but for now it is a bridge, the last Oreo a small treaty between them, as the highway pulls them forward toward Grandma’s, mile by mile.
